Full year 2025 results | 22Regulatory period 5 (power) in Germany: Latest information on methodology setup for the period 2029 – 2033 Cost of Equity (40%) Cost of Debt (60%) Benchmarking OPEX-adjustment mechanism All assets (existing assets + new investments) → Risk-free rate is determined based on a historical average aligned with the length of the regulatory period, using yields from 10– 20-year zero-coupon German or comparable European government bonds → Market Risk Premium based on arithmetic mean of historically realized differences of market returns vs. long-term government bond yields.

All assets (existing assets + new investments) → The regulatory cost of debt is based on sector-specific bond indices with predominantly ten-year maturities, and the associated credit rating must reflect both the risk profile of German network operators and their financing structure.
Existing assets → The cost of debt is fixed for the duration of the regulatory period and is derived from the relevant bond index data on a historical average over the past seven years.
→ A weighting that reflects the sector’s investment behavior over the past seven years can be used in this context1.

New investments: → The cost of debt is dynamically determined using the annual value of the relevant yield series in the year of investment according to the existing cost of capital adjustment methodology in RP4.

→ Move from a so-called “Best-of-4 method”2 to a “Best-of-Mean-of-method”3 → Benchmarking requirements will include redispatch costs as per current proposal → RP5 will include a standardized OPEX adjustment mechanism.

→ This mechanism should reflect energy transition related increases in operating costs occurring after the base year in the revenue cap, based on changes in structural parameters used in the efficiency benchmark.
→ We expect further details on the mechanism by the end of Q1.
(1) A weighting is applied only if significantly higher investment volumes are observed across all network operators within a sector.

(2) Selects as the final efficiency value the best one of 4 efficiency scores, which are calculated using DEA (Data Envelopment Analysis) and SFA (Stochastic Frontier Analysis) methods, each applied to both actual (TOTEX) and standardized (s TOTEX) cost data.
(3) Uses for the efficiency value the better of the two method (DEA, SFA) averages.
